Home ›Symbols ›Scarab

Scarab Symbolism & Meaning

Few symbols carry their archaeological pedigree as cleanly as the scarab. It comes to us already interpreted — pressed into faience, carved into the breast amulets of mummies, inscribed with the prayers of a civilisation that watched a dung beetle and saw the architecture of the cosmos. To read the scarab seriously is to read Egypt first, and only then to follow the symbol forward.

The core reading: rebirth at the threshold

At its most consistent, the scarab is interpreted as an emblem of regeneration — not vague self-improvement, but the specific motion of something dying and returning. The Egyptian observation was unusually literal: Scarabaeus sacer, the dung beetle, rolls a sphere of dung across the desert floor, buries it, and from that buried sphere new beetles appear to emerge as if from nothing. To an agricultural civilisation already obsessed with the sun's daily death and dawn return, this was not metaphor. It was confirmation in miniature.

From this observation came Khepri, the scarab-headed god whose name derives from the verb kheper — "to come into being", "to become". Khepri was the morning sun, the form Ra took as he rolled the solar disc up from the underworld each dawn. The scarab therefore came to symbolise not rebirth in the abstract, but rebirth as labour — something pushed uphill, against gravity, by an insistent and patient force. The reading that survives best is this: the scarab tends to appear when something in the inner life is being slowly, unglamorously rolled back toward the light.

Where many symbols of renewal lean on bloom and flowering, the scarab leans on excrement and burial. This is what gives it its peculiar honesty. The reading many traditions converge on is that real regeneration begins in what was discarded, what was thought worthless, what was left to decompose. The beetle does not avoid the dung; the beetle composes the future from it.

Cultural lineage: from the Nile outward

The scarab's home is Egypt, and most other appearances are downstream tributaries of that source. Heart scarabs — large stone amulets placed over the chest of the deceased — were inscribed with Spell 30B of the Book of the Dead, an instruction to the heart not to testify against its owner at the weighing before Osiris. The scarab here is a guardian at the most consequential threshold imaginable: the judgement of the soul. To wear or carry a scarab was to align oneself with the part of the cosmos that returns.

In Greek and Roman antiquity the scarab travelled as a curiosity and an import. Greek writers including Plutarch recorded Egyptian beliefs about the beetle, sometimes with admiration, sometimes with the slight condescension of cultures that prefer their gods anthropomorphic. Roman legionaries occasionally wore scarab intaglios, often inherited or copied from Egyptian originals, treating them as protective stones. The symbol's gravity travelled even when its theology did not.

In Christian-era Europe the scarab largely disappeared from devotional use but lingered in alchemical and Hermetic imagery, where the motif of something emerging from putrefaction (the nigredo stage) shares an obvious family resemblance with the beetle and its buried sphere. The alchemists rarely cited Khepri by name, but the structural logic — life arising from decay — is recognisably the same lineage.

Modern revivals tend to flatten the symbol into "good luck charm" or generic Egyptiana, which is honest enough as decoration but loses almost everything that made the scarab matter. The serious reading requires holding the original register: this is a symbol about death, about the underworld, about the labour of return.

The Jungian register: Jung's own scarab

The scarab occupies an unusual place in depth psychology because Jung himself wrote about one. In Synchronicity: An Acausal Connecting Principle, he describes a patient locked in over-rationalised resistance, recounting a dream of being given a golden scarab. As she spoke, Jung heard a tapping at the window — a rose chafer, the closest European analogue to the Egyptian scarab, trying to get in. He opened the window, caught the beetle, and handed it to her with the words "here is your scarab." The patient's defences broke; the analysis moved.

Jung took this not as paranormal proof but as a vivid instance of meaningful coincidence — the outer world momentarily mirroring an inner image with enough precision to crack a defensive structure. For Jung the scarab thereafter carried associations with the self-renewing centre of the psyche, the part that knows it must change before the ego does. When a scarab surfaces in dream or active imagination, the Jungian reading is often that the individuation process is moving — slowly, beetle-paced, but moving — and that the conscious attitude is being asked to make room for it.

Variations

The scarab takes many forms across material culture and dreamlife, and the specifics genuinely matter.

Golden scarab. Often read as the symbol at its most concentrated — the solar, individuating self. The colour gold links it explicitly to Ra and to the alchemical goal, suggesting a renewal that is not just survival but transmutation.

Heart scarab. The funerary amulet placed over the chest. In dream or vision it tends to be interpreted as concerning conscience, integrity, and how one will be weighed by one's own deeper self — less about death than about honest self-accounting.
